snippet.title: Depeche Mode: How The Faith and Devotion Tour Nearly killed the Band
snippet.description:
The tour that nearly killed Depeche Mode in 1993

SIGN UP for 10 of the Craziest Stories in Rock N' Roll [Secret Playlist]: https://bit.ly/3vVPAEF

Check out our Top 25 Favourite Albums Here 
https://rockandrolltruestories.com/

Have a video request or a topic you'd like to see us cover? Fill out our google form!
https://bit.ly/3stnXlN

-----CONNECT ON SOCIAL----- 
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/rocknrolltruestories
Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/RNRTrueStories
Twitter: https://twitter.com/rocktruestories
Blog: www.rockandrolltruestories.com

#depechemode #davidgahan

I cite my sources and they may differ than other people's accounts, so I don't guarantee the actual accuracy of my videos.

In 1993 Depeche Mode would release their album songs of faith and devotion. The album was released against the backdrop of alternative rock which was popular at the time and had some influence on the sound of the record. The recording of the album and the tour to support the album tested the band’s career, nearly resulting in David Gahan nearly dying during one live show.  Today let’s take a look at what happened. 

The writing sessions for Depeche Mode’s Songs of Faith and Devotion were full of tensions. The band’s previous record 1990’s Violator was a huge commercial success giving the band their first platinum record in America. 1993 Faith and Devotion would debut at the number 1 spot on the billboard charts in America. Prior to recording teh record the band members had usually not lived together during the recording of their past albums. That changed with Songs of Faith and devotion where the members lived together in Madrid,Hamburg and London. The band’s personal problems and living in close quarters exacerbated internal tensions. Frontman David Gahan was dealing with heroin addiction, while principal songwriter guitarist and keyboardist martin gore was feeling the pressure of following the success of the band’s previous record. Keyboardist Andy Fletcher meanwhile was dealing with depression. The band’s initial sessions had to be scrapped and producer Flood described the recording of the album as and i quote ‘pulling teeth.’ Fletcher meanwhile would recall about the album “We were in the worst possible state as members but we were creating some of our best work… at the time, it was a living hell…I remember thinking ‘I’m never going to make another record under these circumstances again, because it’s so much not fun’. And music should be fun – there should be some sort of enjoyment there.”The band members soon bickered over pushing their own song ideas. Despite the internal tensions the album would be released on March 22 of 1993 to critical and commercial acclaim. It led the band to launch their biggest and most ambitious tour to date. 

Despite the success of the record the problems that ate at the band during the time in the studio joined them on the road. Fletcher would be replaced mid tour due to life on the road, while Alan Wilder would leave the group in 1995 citing his unhappiness with the statement of the band. , Gore would look back at the trek saying, "I don't think anyone was ever the same after that tour", withQ magazine would later refer to the Devotional Tour as "the most debauched rock tour ever".[31]

During a stop in Montreal, Canada frontman David Gahan who was reportedly suffering from a cold according to the band’s label was having trouble singing. Bandmate Martin Gore would take over and sing a few songs. Gahan would return to do a few more numbers before the band ended teh show early. A show in Ohio would be called off after Gahan cut his hand to the point that he couldn’t hold a microphone stand after trying to open a beer bottle. Perhaps the darkest date on the tour happened on October 8, 1993 during a stop over in New Orleans. Gahan would collapse on stage after suffering a drug induced heart attack. When paramedics arrived on stage the band would play ‘Death’s Door.’   Gahan would tell uncut magazine  "Death was the furthest thing from my mind, to be honest," "I was in so much denial about what was really going on."



The band would limp along until July of 1994. During the final show of the tour Gahan jumped into the crowd only to crack several ribs and suffer internal bleeding after falling 12 feet onto a row of seats. 

In 1997 the band would release their follow up Ultra. In an interview with MTV the band revealed they would not tour to support the album The band did however play one mini-performance at a party for radio-contest winners in LA. MTV would report and i quote “Dave Gahan still wobbling back from drug addiction, the band says it decided not to subject him to the backstage temptations of a full-scale tour. 

Perhaps the lack of touring impacted Ultra’s album sales with mtv reporting at the time that the album debuted on the "Billboard" chart at number 5 and quickly dropped off the charts.

#depechemode #davidgahan

I cite my sources and they may differ than other people's accounts, so I don't guarantee the actual accuracy of my videos.

In 1993 Depeche Mode would release their album songs of faith and devotion. The album was released against the backdrop of alternative rock which was popular at the time and had some influence on the sound of the record. The recording of the album and the tour to support the album tested the band’s career, nearly resulting in David Gahan nearly dying during one live show.  Today let’s take a look at what happened. 

The writing sessions for Depeche Mode’s Songs of Faith and Devotion were full of tensions. The band’s previous record 1990’s Violator was a huge commercial success giving the band their first platinum record in America. 1993 Faith and Devotion would debut at the number 1 spot on the billboard charts in America. Prior to recording teh record the band members had usually not lived together during the recording of their past albums. That changed with Songs of Faith and devotion where the members lived together in Madrid,Hamburg and London. The band’s personal problems and living in close quarters exacerbated internal tensions. Frontman David Gahan was dealing with heroin addiction, while principal songwriter guitarist and keyboardist martin gore was feeling the pressure of following the success of the band’s previous record. Keyboardist Andy Fletcher meanwhile was dealing with depression. The band’s initial sessions had to be scrapped and producer Flood described the recording of the album as and i quote ‘pulling teeth.’ Fletcher meanwhile would recall about the album “We were in the worst possible state as members but we were creating some of our best work… at the time, it was a living hell…I remember thinking ‘I’m never going to make another record under these circumstances again, because it’s so much not fun’. And music should be fun – there should be some sort of enjoyment there.”The band members soon bickered over pushing their own song ideas. Despite the internal tensions the album would be released on March 22 of 1993 to critical and commercial acclaim. It led the band to launch their biggest and most ambitious tour to date. 

Despite the success of the record the problems that ate at the band during the time in the studio joined them on the road. Fletcher would be replaced mid tour due to life on the road, while Alan Wilder would leave the group in 1995 citing his unhappiness with the statement of the band. , Gore would look back at the trek saying, "I don't think anyone was ever the same after that tour", withQ magazine would later refer to the Devotional Tour as "the most debauched rock tour ever".[31]

During a stop in Montreal, Canada frontman David Gahan who was reportedly suffering from a cold according to the band’s label was having trouble singing. Bandmate Martin Gore would take over and sing a few songs. Gahan would return to do a few more numbers before the band ended teh show early. A show in Ohio would be called off after Gahan cut his hand to the point that he couldn’t hold a microphone stand after trying to open a beer bottle. Perhaps the darkest date on the tour happened on October 8, 1993 during a stop over in New Orleans. Gahan would collapse on stage after suffering a drug induced heart attack. When paramedics arrived on stage the band would play ‘Death’s Door.’   Gahan would tell uncut magazine  "Death was the furthest thing from my mind, to be honest," "I was in so much denial about what was really going on."



The band would limp along until July of 1994. During the final show of the tour Gahan jumped into the crowd only to crack several ribs and suffer internal bleeding after falling 12 feet onto a row of seats. 

In 1997 the band would release their follow up Ultra. In an interview with MTV the band revealed they would not tour to support the album The band did however play one mini-performance at a party for radio-contest winners in LA. snippet.title: How to Become a TV Writer: Tips from a Co-Executive Producer of THE SIMPSONS
snippet.description:
Dream of becoming a tv writer or a screenwriter? Wondering about tv writing and development? Curious about a showrunner career path? Then you don’t want to miss this video!

In this brief career advice, Allan McKay interviews the Co-Executive Producer and Writer on THE SIMPSONS – as well as the Showrunner of F IS FOR FAMILY – Michael Price.

Michael is a Writers Guild Award and three-time Emmy Award-winning Writer and Producer. He has writer over 20 episodes of THE SIMPSONS, as well as serves as a script consultant.

From how to break into the film industry, to career advice for tv writers, to a showrunner career path, Michael speaks from his experience. He talks about his beginnings in improv, to his first jobs in tv, to the big break of landing THE SIMPSONS writing position. He also shares some insight on the characters like Homer and Marge. And of course, as a veteran of the industry, Michael shares his dread of the empty page and how to deal with the writer's block.

Whether you’re a fan of THE SIMPSONS or Bill Burr’s F IS FOR FAMILY or you’re aspiring to become a tv writer – check out this fun interview with Michael Price. This showrunner interview will not leave you uninspired!

snippet.title: Permission Granted I Season Two, Episode 12
snippet.description:
We are wrapping up season 2 (already!) and this conversation is the perfect way to end our conversation on Finding Purpose. 

When you start dating someone, or are thinking about getting married, it’s pretty common to make sure the people closest to you (especially family) give input on whether your partner is a good fit or not.  Different cultures have different ways of giving “permission” before two families get connected.

But when it comes to stepping into your purpose, do you need “permission”? 

Do you need to wait for things to look good? 
For you to feel ready? 
Or for your next right step to match what other people approve of?

Jesus went before us and occupied different spaces, which gives us permission to do the same. He is our ultimate permission-giver. We are firmly secure in Christ, and no one’s approval or rejection, no uncomfortable or risky space – nothing can take away that security we have in Him. 

Listen in as Heather kicks off this episode with a rapid fire Q+A with Candace, and ask yourself  - would you rather reveal your Netflix watch list or your Music playlists?!

Thank you Heather for being part of this season, and offering a wealth of wisdom and encouragement each week! 

In this episode, you will hear Candace and Heather discuss: 
Needing (or waiting for) permission to take a step into a new space or a next step in life  
How God gives us permission to step into our purpose, even if it doesn’t look perfect or we feel ready 
Spaces Jesus had to occupy so that we could have permission to occupy our own spaces 

Head to Candace.com to download the FREE guide, and to order Heather’s latest book, Right Where You Belong. 

For all things mentioned in this episode head over to 
https://ccbpodcast.com/episodes/season-2/012

TruPlay: TruPlay.com

Each season of The Candace Cameron Bure Podcast features one guest for a deep exploration of a single theme, encouraging viewers to grow in their experiences of faith, family and purposeful living.
---
Best known as ‘D.J Tanner’ in the hit television series “Full House” and Netflix’s “Fuller House,” and as the actor and director who takes over your television around Christmas time.  Candace is also a New York Times bestselling author, entrepreneur, and CEO of Candy Rock Entertainment. Candace has been interviewed many times over her decades on television, and decided it was time to host deep conversations about the many topics people find important.
